coredata.dc:title: Alleviation of dry eye syndrome with one dose of antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and mucoadhesive lysine-carbonized nanogels

Li, Yu-Jia
Huang, Yu-Fen
Anand, Anisha
Huang, Chih-Ching
Lin, Han-Jia
Lai, Jui-Yang
coredata.dc:description:
Most dry eye syndromes (DES) are caused by oxidative stress and an overactive inflammatory response, leading to tear deficiency and excessive tear evaporation. Conventional eye drops for DES treatment require high doses and frequent administration due to their insufficient precorneal residence time. To overcome these problems, in this study, we have developed carbonized nanogels (CNGs) via the straightforward pyrolysis of lysine hydrochloride (Lys) to provide a long-lasting eye drop formulation for topical DES therapy. This methodology thermally converts Lys-into nitrogen-doped crosslinked polymers with embedded nanographitic structures, which enable efficient free radical scavenging. The cationic and crosslinked polymeric features of the Lys-CNGs also prolong the precorneal retention time and improve ocular bioavailability. These Lys-CNGs exhibit high biocompatibility with corneal epithelial cells both in vitro and in vivo, indicating their safety as eye drops. In a DES rabbit model, a single dose of Lys-CNGs (50 µg mL−1) can effectively alleviate the signs of DES within 4 days, whereas multiple treatments of 10-fold higher concentration of cyclosporine A are needed to achieve similar therapeutic effects (one dose every 12 h; 500 µg mL−1). The topical administration of Lys-CNGs enable a reduced therapeutic dose and extended dosing interval, thereby demonstrating a superior therapeutic efficacy compared to the commercial cyclosporine A eye drops. These Lys-CNGs, which exhibit significant free radical scavenging, anti-inflammatory activity, high biocompatibility, and a remarkable ocular bioadhesive property, hold great potential as a long-lasting eye drop formulation for the treatment of dry eye disease.
Statement of significance Multifunctional nanobiomaterial-based eye drops can render an ideal pharmaceutical formulation for the treatment of a variety of ocular surface diseases. To our knowledge, this is the first report describing the development of carbonized nanogels as topically administered therapeutics for alleviating dry eye syndrome (DES). We present evidence that the thermal transformation of lysine hydrochloride into carbonized nanogels (Lys-CNGs) endows superior antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and bioadhesive properties. While a single dose of Lys-CNGs (50 µg mL−1) is sufficient to relieve the symptoms of DES for 4 days, multiple treatments of 10-fold higher concentration of commercially available cyclosporine eye drops are needed to achieve similar therapeutic outcomes (one dose every 12 h; 500 µg mL−1), suggesting an effective and long-lasting ocular carbonized nanomedicine.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Size, morphology, and optical properties of Lys-CNGs. (A) TEM images and (B) UV-visible absorption spectra of Lys-CNGs-240, Lys-CNGs-260, and Lys-CNGs-280. Inset to (A) HRTEM images of Lys-CNGs and (B) the photographs of the corresponding solutions.

Free radical scavenging ability of Lys-CNGs. DPPH· (100 µg mL−1) incubated with ascorbic acid (0 − 25 µg mL−1), lysine hydrochloride (0.05−10 mg mL−1), poly-lysine (0.05−10 mg mL−1) or Lys-CNGs (0 − 50 µg mL−1) for 30 min. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three repeated experiments.

Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects of Lys-CNGs in SIRC cells. (A) Fluorescence images (upper panel) of the SIRC cells incubated with different Lys-CNGs (50 µg mL−1) for 12 h followed by exposure to H2O2 (350 µM) for 6 h, and imaged after DCFH-DA treatment. The cells receiving PBS without treatment with H2O2 and tested materials serve as a control (Ctrl) group. Intracellular levels of ROS (lower panel) were quantified by measuring the fluorescence intensity of DCF with maximum excitation/emission wavelengths of 495/529 nm. (B) TNF-α (upper panel) and IL-6 (lower panel) levels of the SIRC cells incubated with different Lys-CNGs for 12 h followed by LPS (2.0 µg mL−1) stimulation for 12 h. The cells receiving PBS without treatment with H2O2, LPS, and tested materials serve as a control (Ctrl) group. Scale bar in (A): 100 µm. Error bars represent the standard deviation from the mean of three repeated experiments. Asterisks indicate statistically significant differences of the Ctrl or treated groups as compared to the H2O2 (A) or LPS group (B and C) (* p < 0.05, ⁎⁎ p < 0.01, ⁎⁎⁎ p < 0.001). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Therapeutic efficacy of Lys-CNGs verified by corneal staining. One dose of Lys-CNGs can mitigate the benzalkonium chloride-induced corneal epithelial damage in rabbits. The upper panel shows the time course of different treatments. After treatment with CsA (0.005, 0.05% w/w; one dose at 12 h) or Lys-CNGs (50 µg mL−1, one dose per 12 h, 48 h and 96 h) in DES rabbits, slit-lamp biomicroscopic images of rabbit eyes were stained with (A) fluorescein and (B) rose Bengal (upper panels). Lower panels of (A) and (B) show the respective corneal staining scores. Error bars represent the standard deviation from the mean of three repeated experiments. Scale bar: 7.5 mm. Asterisks indicate statistically significant differences of the treated groups as compared to the Ctrl group (* p < 0.001, ⁎⁎ p < 0.00001). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Histological analyses. (A) H&E staining of corneal tissue, (B) corneal epithelial thickness, (C) TUNEL fluorescence images of corneal tissue, and (D) relative TUNEL area of the cornea before (control) and after inducing DES followed by treatment with CsA or Lys-CNGs-260 (one dose per 12, 48 and 96 h). Scale bar: 50 µm. Error bars represent the standard deviation from the mean of three repeated experiments. Asterisks indicate statistically significant differences of the treated or control groups as compared to the DES groups (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.005, ***p < 0.001). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Impression cytology on conjunctiva and mucin expression. (A) Conjunctival impression cytological images showing the goblet cells, (B) the corresponding goblet cell number, and (C) MUC5AC immune fluorescence images, and (D) relative MUC5AC area of conjunctiva before (control) and after inducing DES followed by treatment with CsA or Lys-CNGs-260 (one dose per 12, 48 and 96 h). Scale bar: 40 µm (A) and 100 µm (C). Error bars represent the standard deviation from the mean of three repeated experiments. Asterisks indicate statistically significant differences of the treated or control groups as compared to DES groups (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.005, ***p < 0.001). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Schematic representation of the one-step synthesis of lysine-carbonized nanogels (Lys-CNGs) and their application for the treatment of dry eye disease through long-term mucoadhesion on the ocular surface to exert antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Most dry eye syndromes (DES) are caused by oxidative stress and an overactive inflammatory response, leading to tear deficiency and excessive tear evaporation. Conventional eye drops for DES treatment require high doses and frequent administration due to their insufficient precorneal residence time. To overcome these problems, in this study, we have developed carbonized nanogels (CNGs) via the straightforward pyrolysis of lysine hydrochloride (Lys) to provide a long-lasting eye drop formulation for topical DES therapy. This methodology thermally converts Lys-into nitrogen-doped crosslinked polymers with embedded nanographitic structures, which enable efficient free radical scavenging. The cationic and crosslinked polymeric features of the Lys-CNGs also prolong the precorneal retention time and improve ocular bioavailability. These Lys-CNGs exhibit high biocompatibility with corneal epithelial cells both in vitro and in vivo, indicating their safety as eye drops. In a DES rabbit model, a single dose of Lys-CNGs (50 µg mL−1) can effectively alleviate the signs of DES within 4 days, whereas multiple treatments of 10-fold higher concentration of cyclosporine A are needed to achieve similar therapeutic effects (one dose every 12 h; 500 µg mL−1). The topical administration of Lys-CNGs enable a reduced therapeutic dose and extended dosing interval, thereby demonstrating a superior therapeutic efficacy compared to the commercial cyclosporine A eye drops. These Lys-CNGs, which exhibit significant free radical scavenging, anti-inflammatory activity, high biocompatibility, and a remarkable ocular bioadhesive property, hold great potential as a long-lasting eye drop formulation for the treatment of dry eye disease.

Multifunctional nanobiomaterial-based eye drops can render an ideal pharmaceutical formulation for the treatment of a variety of ocular surface diseases. To our knowledge, this is the first report describing the development of carbonized nanogels as topically administered therapeutics for alleviating dry eye syndrome (DES). We present evidence that the thermal transformation of lysine hydrochloride into carbonized nanogels (Lys-CNGs) endows superior antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and bioadhesive properties. While a single dose of Lys-CNGs (50 µg mL−1) is sufficient to relieve the symptoms of DES for 4 days, multiple treatments of 10-fold higher concentration of commercially available cyclosporine eye drops are needed to achieve similar therapeutic outcomes (one dose every 12 h; 500 µg mL−1), suggesting an effective and long-lasting ocular carbonized nanomedicine.

ce:section.ce:para: Dry eye syndrome (DES) is a multifactorial ocular disease affecting millions of people worldwide. It is accompanied by increased osmolarity and inflammation on the ocular surface, leading to insufficient tear secretion and excessive evaporation [1,2]. The bioenvironmental imbalance may further cause discomfort, signs of visual disturbance, and damage to ocular surface [3,4]. In the pathogenesis of dry eye, pro-oxidant conditions predominate due to increased free radicals and/or low quenching/scavenging efficiency. The excessive accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and tissue inflammation can also directly contribute to the presence of ocular surface alteration, resulting in the loss of goblet cells, reduced mucin production, decreased corneal wettability, abnormality of epithelial cell, and immune dysregulation [2,5-7]. In addition, infiltration of immune cells is known to affect the function of the ocular surface, lacrimal gland, and meibomian glands. Overexpression of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1β, IL-6, IL-17, IFN-γ, and TNF-α in immune cells has been documented in patients with DES [8,9]. In short, oxidative stress and inflammation play an important role in the mechanism of DES.

In addition to artificial tears, other common medications to DES include topical cyclosporine A (CsA) to mediate immune response suppression as well as topical non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) and steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (SAIDs) to rein in inflammation [10]. Unfortunately, some treatments may cause unwanted side effects. For example, NSAIDs (e.g., bromfenac, ketorolac tromethamine, and nepafenac) may lead to complications such as ulceration and corneal perforation, while SAIDs (e.g., difluprednate, fluorometholone, loteprednol etobonate, and prednisolone) have been reported to induce glaucoma and cataract formation [10,11]. Moreover, eye drops usually retain shortly on the ocular surface due to frequent blinking, tearing, and tear drainage into nasolacrimal duct [12,13]. Poor bioavailability of ocular drugs (<5%) requires frequent administration (several times a day), which can exacerbate the side effects and even damage the eyes [14]. To address these issues, various formulations such as ointments, emulsions, and hydrogels have been developed to reduce the dosing frequency and maintain effective drug concentrations for topical application [15,16]. However, ointment formulation is prone to cause blurred vision, and emulsion requires a high amount of surfactant, which increases the risk of cytotoxicity [17]. The recent advances in ocular drug delivery mainly focus on the use of colloidal drug delivery systems, such as liposomes, polymers, and mesoporous nanoparticles [18]. Although promising, nanoparticle-based delivery systems require complex and tedious preparation, drug loading, and purification procedures. Undesirable drug release behavior may result in initial burst release and relatively short therapeutic lifetime. The biocompatibility of artificial nanocarriers is also a concern. So far, a large number of ophthalmic drug delivery systems have been reported in the literature, but relatively few have been commercialized.

According to recent studies, carbon-based nanomaterials are characterized by their ability to promote electron transfer reactions [19–21]. Many carbon nanomaterials can act as electron donors or acceptors, thereby enabling their use as pro-oxidants or antioxidants [19]. In terms of antioxidant capacity, it is mainly due to the presence of functional groups such as carboxyl or amino groups on the surface that participate in direct or indirect hydrogen atom transfer reactions [20]. Some studies have also reported that the radical scavenging activity is related to the sp2 carbon network, which enables delocalization, conjugation and formation of secondary adducts [21]. In addition, heteroatom doping on carbon nanomaterials greatly enhances the antioxidant performance due to the increase in electron density. Previous studies have also shown that the type and amount of heteroatoms contribute to varying antioxidant properties [22,23]. Given this, carbon-based nanomaterials with inherent biocompatibility and remarkable antioxidant activity have broad prospects as candidate nanodrugs for ocular disease treatment [24]. Lysine and polymers derived from it have been reported as antioxidant and anti-inflammatory agents [25–27]. Our group has reported carbonized nanogels (CNGs) from quercetin and lysine with antibacterial and antioxidant properties for the treatment of bacterial keratitis [24]. CNGs from lysine obtained by controlled pyrolysis have also been reported as a broad-spectrum antibacterial agent to tackle drug resistance [28]. These CNGs also possess virus inhibition properties through preventing the attachment of the virus to the host cells, and were effective against infectious bronchitis virus [29].

In this study, we have synthesized carbonized nanogels (Lys-CNGs) with strong antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, through a controlled pyrolysis of lysine hydrochloride, as long-lasting eye drops for DES treatment (Scheme 1 ). The free radical scavenging ability associated with the carbonized structures of CNGs was thoroughly investigated. Lys-CNGs exhibited high biocompatibility in both in vitro and in vivo experiments. The topical eye-drops formulation made of Lys-CNGs can effectively alleviate DES in rabbit eye model. Lys-CNGs can prolong the retention time on the ocular surface through strong interaction with the mucin layer due to their cationic nature. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study of drug-free carbonized nanomaterials that can be applied to treat DES by exerting good antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities.

The lysine-carbonized nanogels (Lys-CNGs) were prepared from lysine hydrochloride by a thermal polymerization and carbonization method. In a typical process, 1 mL of deionized water was added to 300 mg of lysine hydrochloride in a 20 mL glass vial and heated in a muffle furnace at temperatures of 240, 260, or 280 °C for 3 h and cooled to room temperature. 10 mL of deionized water was added to the glass vials containing the pyrolyzed product and sonicated for 1 h and centrifuged at a relative centrifugal force (RCF) of 5000 g for 30 min. The Lys-CNGs dispersed in the supernatant were collected and dialyzed against deionized water for 5 h, with the water replaced every hour. The obtained products were denoted as Lys-CNGs-T (T = 240, 260, or 280 °C). The Lys-CNGs were stored at –20 °C for future use.

TEM images of the Lys-CNGs were obtained using a Tecnai 20 G2 S-Twin transmission electron microscope (Philips/FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA). Fluorescence and UV–visible absorption spectra of the as-prepared CNGs were recorded using a monochromatic microplate spectrophotometer (Synergy 4 Multi-Mode; BioTek Instruments, Winooski, VT, USA).

The free radical scavenging ability of the Lys-CNGs was evaluated by 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH·) radical scavenging assay [30]. Briefly, Lys-CNGs (0–50 µg mL−1) were mixed with DPPH· (100 µg mL−1) in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) at room temperature. After 30 min, the percentage DPPH· scavenging effect was measured by a monochromatic microplate spectrophotometer (Infinite M200, TECAN, Switzerland) at 515 nm.

Statens Seruminstitut Rabbit Cornea (SIRC) cells (BCRC 60093) were obtained from the Bioresource Collection and Research Center (Hsinchu, Taiwan, ROC). The SIRC cells were grown in a culture medium containing Minimum Essential Medium (MEM), 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% antibiotic/antimycotic (A/A) solution in 5% CO2 at 37 °C. The cell viability and cell number were determined by MTT assay and trypan blue exclusion method, respectively. SIRC cells (3.5 × 104 cells per well) were seeded into 48-well culture plates (200 µL well−1) and incubated for 24 h in the presence of 5% CO2 at 37 °C. Then the culture media in each well was replaced with 200 µL of fresh medium containing different concentration (1 − 100 µg mL−1) of Lys-CNGs. After 48 h culture, the cells were rinsed three times with PBS, and treated with the MTT reagent and incubated for 4 h (5% CO2, 37 °C). The cell survival rate was analyzed by measuring the absorbance at 570 nm using a monochromatic microplate spectrophotometer.

The cellular antioxidant ability of Lys-CNGs was determined by hydrogen peroxide (H2O2)-induced oxidative stress of the SIRC cells. The SIRC cells (3.5 × 104 cells per well) were seeded in 48-well culture plates and incubated with Lys-CNGs in cell culture medium for 12 h. The Lys-CNGs in the cell culture were then removed and further incubated for 6 h in medium with 350 µM H2O2. Intracellular accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) was measured by 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) assays. The SIRC cells were then incubated with 20 µM DCFH-DA solutions at 37 °C for 30 min and the green fluorescence imaging was performed with a fluorescence microscope (infinite M200, TECAN, Switzerland). The fluorescence intensity was also measured with a monochromatic microplate spectrophotometer with excitation and emission wavelength of 480 and 525 nm, respectively.

The SIRC cells were seeded in a 48-well plate (2 × 10 4 cells per well) and incubated in MEM overnight for cell attachment. Then, Lys-CNGs (1 − 50 µg mL−1) were added after removing the medium. After 12 h of incubation, the supernatant was removed and lipopolysaccharide (LPS) (2.0 µg mL−1) was added into the wells to induce inflammation of SIRC cells. After 12 h of incubation, the supernatant was collected and reconstituted in ELISA buffer and separately assayed for TNF-α and IL-6 by competitive ELISA kit (R&D Systems, Minneapolis, MN, USA).

For all the in vivo experiments, the animal use protocols were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Chang Gung University (IACUC Approval No.: CGU109–185). All animal procedures were performed in accordance with the ARVO Statement for the Use of Animals in Ophthalmic and Vision Research. For therapeutic evaluation of Lys-CNGs, first, an experimental DES model was induced in rabbit eyes by topical administration of 0.15% benzalkonium chloride twice daily for 14 days [31]. A total of six DES rabbit groups were studied including one control and five test groups. Each group consisted of six animals. The group receiving 50 µL of PBS alone (without any drugs or nanomaterials) served as a control (Ctrl), whereas 50 µL of ophthalmic solutions containing 50 µg mL−1 Lys-CNGs were topically administrated to the DES test groups according to the following regimen: group 1 − eight doses over four consecutive days (12 h interval); group 2 − two doses, one each on day 0 and 2 (48 h interval) and group 3 − one dose on day 0, with the first dose administered to all groups at the same time. For comparison, CsA (0.05%), the commercial eye drop Restasis® (Allergan, Inc., Irvine, CA) was administered at a dose of 50 µL twice daily for 4 consecutive days. Clinical and histological examinations of the treated DES eyes were carried out daily for 4 days. The changes in the ocular surface during the treatment was observed and graded by means of slit-lamp biomicroscopy. Staining assays using corneal fluorescein and rose bengal staining via topical application (2 µL of 1% fluorescein sodium or rose bengal) into the conjunctival sac was used to evaluate the DES level and observe the changes in tear film on the cornea. For slit-lamp microscopy evaluation, the fluorescence intensity on the cornea was quantified using a cobalt blue light. A standardized 4-point scale (0 = none, 1 = mild, 2 = moderate, and 3 = severe) was applied in each of three areas for the staining analysis [32]. The total scores ranged from 0 to 9. The tear production was determined using Schirmer tear test strip (Color Bar Schirmer Tear Test, EagleVision, Memphis, TN, USA) without topical anesthesia. The paper strip was inserted into the external third of the lower eyelid for 5 min and the wetted length was recorded as Schirmer score in millimeters.

For the evaluation of the therapeutic effect of Lys-CNGs-260, at the end of experiments, the rabbits were sacrificed, and the cornea were collected for histological examinations. Specimens were fixed in paraformaldehyde, dehydrated in a graded ethanol series, and embedded in parafilm. Thin sections (5 µm) were obtained and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) before examination under a light microscope. In addition to corneal epithelial thickness, apoptotic cell number was analyzed by TUNEL assay. Following fixation and permeabilization, the samples were incubated with a mixture of TdT solution and fluorescein isothiocyanate dUTP solution in a humidity chamber for 1 h at 37 °C. Negative controls were treated with distilled water in place of TdT enzyme. Slides were counterstained with DAPI for the identification of cell nuclei. Under a fluorescence microscope, the number of TUNEL-positive apoptotic cell nuclei was counted at three randomly selected fields. Circular disks of nitrocellulose filter paper were placed on the nasal and temporal bulbar conjunctiva to collect conjunctival impression cytology specimens. Samples were further stained with hematoxylin and periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) for the identification of goblet cells. The specimens were fixed by formalin-ethanol solution for 1 min, rinsed in distilled water, and then treated with periodic acid solution for 5 min. After rinsing with distilled water, the specimens were treated with Schiff's Reagent for 15 min, again rinsed with distilled water, and stained with Hematoxylin solution for 90 s. The samples were further rinsed, air-dried, and observed under a microscope. Under the light microscope, the number of goblet cells was counted at three randomly selected fields and averaged (cells/high-power [HP] visual field with 400×). The tissue sections of the nasal and temporal bulbar conjunctiva were processed for immunohistochemical staining of MUC5AC. Following fixation and blocking procedures, the samples were incubated with mouse anti-rabbit MUC5AC antibody (1:150) overnight at 4 °C. Negative controls were treated without primary antibody. After washing in PBS, the specimens were incubated with fluorescein-conjugated secondary antibody (1:200; Chemicon International, Temecula, CA, USA) for 1 h at room temperature in the dark. Slides were counterstained with DAPI and examined under fluorescence microscope. The number of MUC5AC-positive cells was counted at three randomly selected fields and averaged (cells/high-power [HP] visual field with 400×).

All in vitro data were presented as mean ± SD from three independent experiments. The animal model experimental results were presented as mean ± SD from 6 individual experiments. Differences between mean values were analyzed with ANOVA followed by the Student's t-test. P-values less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.
See the Supporting Information for the details on chemicals, characterization of Lys-CNGs, mucoadhesion studies, in vivo biocompatibility, and ocular-retention studies.

Lys-CNGs were prepared by pyrolyzing lysine hydrochloride (in water, 300 mg mL–1) at 240, 260 or 280 °C for 3 h. The obtained Lys-CNGs were dispersed in deionized water (10 mL) and displayed good aqueous solubility after sonicating for 1 h. The yields of Lys-CNGs-240, Lys-CNGs-260, and Lys-CNGs-280 were 10.4, 19.9, and 21.8%, and the zeta potential (ζ) values were 11.4, 22.8, and 37.3 mV, respectively (Table S1, Supporting Information). The cationic nature of the Lys-CNGs is due to the high amine content, which is preserved during dehydration, condensation (polymerization, cyclization, and crosslinking), and carbonization of lysine by heating [24,27,29]. The pyrolysis of lysine is reported to form residues including pyridine, lactams, piperidines, pyrroles, amides, tertiary amine, hexamethyleneimine, and cyclohexanone, which will be incorporated into Lys-CNGs [33]. The hydrodynamic diameters of Lys-CNGs decreased from ca. 1000 nm to 200 nm with increase in temperature (Table S1, Supporting Information), due to the fragmentation of large structures with increasing temperature.

In the transmission electron microscope (TEM) images, Lys-CNGs-240, Lys-CNGs-260, and Lys-CNGs-280 appeared as nanogel-like structures with sizes of approximately 1000, 600, and 200 nm, respectively (Fig. 1
A). The hydrodynamic diameters of Lys-CNGs measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) were close to the size measured by TEM (Table S1, Supporting Information). The TEM shows the sizes of Lys-CNGs are larger than that determined by DLS, probably because of the aggregation of Lys-CNGs on the copper grid during the TEM sample preparation. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of Lys-CNGs showed varying d -spacing values of 0.26 and 0.33 nm, which are attributed to the (020) and (002) facets of the graphitic structures, respectively, revealing the formation of embedded crystalline graphene-like structures (inset in Fig. 1 A) [34,35]. Also, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Lys-CNGs in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information) show a broad band at 26° (2θ). The corresponding d-spacing was calculated as 0.33 nm, which matched the (002) plane of graphite [35,36]. Furthermore, in Raman spectra, two prominent bands at 1595 and 1362 cm−1 designated as G and D bands become more obvious as Lys-CNGs are prepared at a higher temperature (Fig. S2, Supporting Information). The G and D bands are associated with the in-plane and out-of-plane vibrations of the aromatic carbon rings, representing the presence of graphene-like domains and defective/disordered carbon structures, respectively [34,35].

The UV-visible spectra of Lys-CNGs showed a strong absorption around 300 nm, which may be ascribed to the π→π* transitions of conjugated C = C bonds in graphene-like nanostructures formed by carbonization (Fig. 1 B) [37]. The shoulder band of 330−350 nm is mainly due to the interlayer π→π* charge transfer or n→π* transition of the amino-based chromophores, oxygen (C=O bond)- and nitrogen (C=N bond)-containing functional groups of CNGs [38,39]. Lys-CNGs also exhibited the typical excitation-dependent fluorescence characteristics of carbonized nanomaterials (Fig. S3, Supporting Information). This can be attributed to the crosslinking-enhanced emission effects, different sizes of nitrogen-incorporated polycyclic aromatic clusters, surface emissive traps, and edge defects [40–42].

The elemental composition (C, H, O, N, and Cl) of Lys-CNGs, as presented in Table S2 (Supporting Information), shows that the carbon content of Lys-CNGs increased from 45.2 to 58.2% as the temperature increased, indicating the higher degree of carbonization at higher temperatures. In contrast, the content of oxygen and nitrogen decreased with increasing temperature, which may be due to thermal degradation [24]. The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of Lys-CNGs-240 retained more unique characteristics of free lysine (Fig. S4, Supporting Information), while the signals of C–N stretching (1342–1266 cm–1), C = O stretching (1643 cm–1), and C = C stretching (1630 cm–1) appeared after pyrolysis. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results in Fig. S5 (Supporting Information) also confirmed similar observations. Deconvolution of the C1s spectra of Lys-CNGs reveals four types of carbon bonds, viz. C − C/C = C (284.6 eV), C − N (285.7 eV) and C = O (287.5 eV). The percentages of C‒C/C = C in Lys-CNGs-240, Lys-CNGs-260, and Lys-CNGs-280 are 59.6, 63.3, and 76.5%, respectively, confirming the higher degree of carbonization at elevated temperatures [43]. The N1s spectrum demonstrated four types of nitrogen bonds, viz. C − N (397.5 eV), pyridinic-N (398.4 eV), pyrrolic-N (399.8 eV) and C−NH+ (401.0 eV). Processing at higher temperatures also makes the N-containing heteroaromatic rings more obvious through pyrolytic oxidation and carbonization reactions.

Owing to the positive surface charge and the presence of graphene-like nanostructure consisting of heteroaromatics‒pyridine and pyrrole, the Lys-CNGs could be a potent antioxidant for the treatment of DES [21]. The radical scavenging ability of Lys-CNGs were performed by 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) assay and compared with that of lysine hydrochloride, and poly-lysine (mol. wt. >300,000) (Fig. 2 ). In comparison with ascorbic acid (25 µg mL–1), Lys-CNGs exhibit a slightly weaker radical scavenging ability at the same concentration. Notably, Lys-CNGs are 100-fold stronger than lysine hydrochloride and poly-lysine in scavenging DPPH radicals. The scavenging activity of Lys-CNGs is attributed to the cyclization and carbonization process during the mild pyrolysis, which generates active sites, moieties, and functional groups (e.g., defective or edged graphene, pyrrole, pyridine, and hydroxyl groups) responsible for efficient electron transfer, hydrogen donation, and radical adduct formation [31,44–46]. The π-conjugated system in graphene-like structure of Lys-CNGs improves the storage capability of electrons and acts as electron donors to scavenge free radicals. In addition, abundant amino and ketonic carbonyl surface groups are effective hydrogen donors for stabilizing free radicals. The presence of heteroatoms creates positive or negative charges on adjacent carbon atoms in the graphite lattice. The polarization of carbon atoms thus contributes to enhance the antioxidant performance of Lys-CNGs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potent in vitro antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities
ce:section.ce:para:
Oxidative stress plays a vital role in the pathogenesis of DES. ROS can cause various harmful effects on ocular surface, inducing damage to protein, lipids, DNA and even cell apoptosis [6,47]. The DCFH-DA assay in Fig. 3
A shows the potency of Lys-CNGs in eliminating intracellular ROS in SIRC cells induced by H2O2 stimulation. The Lys-CNGs-260 and Lys-CNGs-280 groups displayed fluorescence patterns similar to that of the Ctrl group, indicating remarkable ROS scavenging ability. However, SIRC cells treated with Lys-CNGs-280 showed abnormal cell morphology (Fig. S6A, Supporting Information), which may be due to the disruption of cellular membrane by the highly positively charged Lys-CNGs-280 (+37.3 mV), compared to Lys-CNGs-240 and Lys-CNGs-260. MTT assay was further performed to determine the cytotoxic effect of Lys-CNGs toward SIRC cells (Fig. S6B, Supporting Information). Lys-CNGs-240 and Lys-CNGs-260 showed negligible toxicity; however, the cell viability decreased to 65% in the presence of Lys-CNGs-280 at a higher concentration of 50 µg mL−1.

Studies have demonstrated that lysine and poly-lysine can attenuate cellular inflammatory response through different pathways such as activating calcium-sensing receptor (CaSR), enhancing ERK1/2 phosphorylation, and suppressing PTEN to promote M2 microglial polarization, thereby inhibiting the expression of the pro-inflammatory cytokines [48]. The two common mechanisms of the pathogenesis of DES ocular surface injury are ocular surface inflammation and increased tear osmotic pressure [49]. Lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced cell inflammation has been reported as a reliable model to study DES [50,51]. Report further shows that topically applied LPS increased expression of inflammatory mediators IL-1β, CXCL10, IL-12a, and IFN-γ in the conjunctiva, and IL-1β and CXCL10 in the cornea of mice [52]. Therefore, we compared the anti-inflammatory effects of Lys-CNGs-240, Lys-CNGs-260, and Lys-CNGs-280 on LPS-stimulated SIRC cells. TNF-α and IL-6 were determined simultaneously to monitor the response against inflammation by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). As depicted in Fig. 3 B, LPS-induced inflammation resulted in >10-fold increase in the levels of TNF-α and IL-6 as compared to the untreated Ctrl groups. The Lys-CNGs-240 and Lys-CNGs-260 showed superior inhibition of TNF-α and IL-6 expression relative to Lys-CNGs-280. In parallel experiments, lysine and poly-lysine at the same concentration did not significantly suppress the oxidative and inflammatory stresses induced by H2O2 and LPS (data not shown). We speculate that the as-formed functional moieties on CNGs under mild pyrolysis conditions play a crucial role in mediating their extraordinary antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities. We will unveil the detailed anti-inflammatory mechanism of Lys-CNGs prepared at different temperatures in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biocompatibility and therapeutic effects on DES
ce:section.ce:para: This study aims to develop a highly biocompatible ophthalmic formulation having antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities for topical administration using Lys-CNGs. Thus, the response of the anterior segment tissue to Lys-CNGs-260 were evaluated by examining the morphological changes in the ocular surface, corneal thickness, corneal endothelial cell morphology, and corneal endothelial cell density of the experimental animals (Fig. S7 and related discussion, Supporting Information). No aqueous flare, neovascularization, corneal and conjunctival edema, iritis, or corneal inflammation were observed, indicating that the Lys-CNGs-260 groups exhibit good ocular biocompatibility. The high in vivo biocompatibility and tolerability of Lys-CNGs-260 allow for their further use in the treatment of DES.

The therapeutic efficacy of the Lys-CNGs-260 drops for topical ocular treatment was assessed by an experimental benzalkonium chloride‒induced DES model. Benzalkonium chloride is a widely used preservative in eye care products, which can accelerate tear film rupture and dryness [31]. Signs of corneal/conjunctival damage and insufficient tear secretion were observed in rabbits afflicted with DES. Screening of ocular surface and tear film can reveal the dry eye conditions, which is a common practice in clinical ophthalmology. It can be evaluated by a Schirmer test value of ≤ 5 mm, fluorescein vital staining score of ≥ 3, and a rose bengal staining score of ≥ 3 [7,53]. Benzalkonium chloride‒induced corneal epithelial defects and tear film rupture are evidenced by the intense fluorescein and rose bengal staining of DES eyes (Fig. S8, Supporting Information) in comparison with the normal group. For the DES rabbits receiving PBS alone (Ctrl groups), staining detected by fluorescein and rose bengal remained positive during the follow-up period (Fig. S9, Supporting Information), indicating that PBS moisturizing is not effective in treating the advanced syndrome of DES with severe inflammation and ROS production. However, rabbits treated with topical Lys-CNGs (50 µg mL−1) (one dose per 12 h) showed a significant reduction in corneal fluorescein- and rose bengal-stained area on day 2 post-treatment. Among the tested Lys-CNGs, Lys-CNGs-260 groups performed best in reducing benzalkonium chloride-induced rabbit corneal epithelial defects and tear film damage. This is also evident from the significant drop in the corneal staining score of the Lys-CNGs-260 treatment groups.

Similarly, the score of rose bengal was greatly improved after Lys-CNGs-260 treatment; the changes observed in the equivalent dose of CsA are negligible (Fig. 4 B). Even in the case of a single administration, the staining score of the Lys-CNGs-260 groups on day 4 post-treatment was significantly different from that of the PBS Ctrl groups, indicating that topical use of Lys-CNGs-260 can effectively repair the tear film (Fig. 4 B, lower panel). In addition, Schirmer's test shows that the benzalkonium chloride-induced DES could cause a significant decrease in Schirmer score (Fig. S10, Supporting Information), which is an indicator of tear secretion. The wetting lengths of the Ctrl (8.2 ± 0.8 mm) and CsA (9.1 ± 0.9 mm) groups (p>0.05) were almost the same, but higher than that of the non-treated DES (5.0 ± 0.4 mm) groups (p<0.05) and significantly lower than the Lys-CNGs-260-treated (one dose per 12, 48 and 96 h) groups (p<0.05). The wetting length of all the Lys-CNGs-260 groups with different dosing frequencies comply with the normal value (>12 mm) [54], showing very mild dry eye. Our results demonstrate that Lys-CNGs-260 exhibited a synergistic therapeutic effect in the management of DES, which can accelerate the healing of damaged epithelium and aqueous layer. However, in this study, CsA could not completely reduce the abnormal preocular tear film and deficient tear production, because commercial CsA usually requires continuous treatment for 6 months to alleviate the inflammation signs associated with DES [55]. While the slow onset of action of commercial CsA may render it a rather poor control for a 4-day experiment, the results nevertheless highlight the advantages of Lys-CNGs-260 compared to the commercial formulation, offering a significant therapeutic advantage.

Long-term retention of Lys-CNGs-260 on the ocular surface could be one of the reasons for their good anti-DES performance, which may overcome the low retention caused by tearing and nasolachrymal drainage. Strong interaction between the nanodrug and the ocular surface may increase its bioavailability. To evaluate the applicability of Lys-CNGs-260 as a mucoadhesive nanodrug, the fluorescence (excitation: 365 nm) of Lys-CNGs-260 on the cornea was measured to determine their mucoadhesive ability and eye retention. The cornea was examined 0.5 − 48 h post-treatment to detect Lys-CNGs-260 on the ocular surface, and the retention rate was calculated as a percentage of the initial use of Lys-CNGs-260. According, the retention of Lys-CNGs-260 in the time course of 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 12 and 48 h was determined to be 76.6 ± 6.8, 45.8 ± 4.2, 21.6 ± 2.5, 13.2 ± 1.4, 7.4 ± 0.5, and 6.5 ± 0.5%, respectively. Compared with ordinary eye drops that retain less than 5% after 10 min [56,57], Lys-CNGs-260 exhibited a longer retention capacity (>5% at 48 h). The improved ocular retention was suggested to be due to the specific interaction between Lys-CNGs-260 and the mucin layer (Fig. S11 and related discussion, Supporting Information) [56]. It should also be noted that the instillation of Lys-CNGs-260 on the cornea did not cause any ocular discomfort or irritation in rabbit eyes (by observing persistent squinting, inability to rest or sleep normally), and no tissue response was observed (Fig. S7), indicating that Lys-CNGs-260 provide sufficient biocompatibility for ophthalmic administration. In view of the remarkable radical scavenging ability, prolonged retention capacity, high bioavailability, and biocompatibility, Lys-CNGs-260 are promising for effective DES treatment at low doses (e.g., 50 µg mL–1) and frequencies (e.g., once per 96 h) compared with previously reported drug-loaded nanocarriers [16,54,58–60].

Histological analyses were performed on tissue specimens collected at the endpoint (day 4) to understand Lys-CNGs-260‒mediated recovery of benzalkonium chloride-induced corneal epithelial injury. The H&E staining and corneal epithelial thickness values are shown in Fig. 5
A and 5 B. In comparison with the Ctrl groups (42.3 ± 8.1 µm), the corneal epithelial layer thickness of DES rabbits was higher (111.7 ± 16.5 µm). The H&E staining of rabbit corneal stroma in Lys-CNGs-260-treated groups exhibited less infiltration of inflammatory cells [61,62]. The corneas treated with Lys-CNGs-260 drops once per 12 h (49.0 ± 8.0 µm), 48 h (54.3 ± 6.1 µm) and 96 h groups (68.0 ± 5.6 µm) showed multilayered epithelium and well-organized stromal lamellae, similar to the Ctrl groups. However, the CsA groups (84.3 ± 14.0 µm) showed incomplete epithelial tissue integrity, indicating an insufficient repair response in the ocular surface. Indeed, it has been reported that benzalkonium chloride, used as a preservative in ophthalmic preparations, can induce inflammation and apoptosis of ocular surface epithelial cells [61,62]. TUNEL assays were performed to further evaluate the DNA fragmentation and cell apoptosis levels of DES rabbits treated with topically administered Lys-CNGs-260 for 4 days (Fig. 5 C). The DES groups showed a large number of apoptotic corneal epithelial cells with strong green fluorescence, while the CsA groups showed a relatively weak signal. All Lys-CNGs-260 groups with different dosing frequencies exhibited a significantly reduced proportion of TUNEL-positive cells even in the case of single administration (one dose per 96 h) (Fig. 5 D). No significant differences were observed between the Ctrl and Lys-CNGs-260‒treated groups, further indicating the strong therapeutic effect of Lys-CNGs-260 on DES treatment.

DES is associated with the loss of cytoprotection due to a defective mucin layer, which necessitates the conjunctival goblet cells to produce mucin to strengthen tear film over the ocular surface [63]. Therefore, the therapeutic outcomes were evaluated by conjunctival impression cytology and MUC5AC immunohistochemistry. The staining of tissue specimens from the Ctrl groups showed intracytoplasmic periodic acid-Schiff (PAS)-positive purple granules (Fig. 6
A) [63]. Very few PAS-positive cells were observed in the DES groups, suggesting that treatment with PBS alone is insufficient to alleviate the depletion of goblet cells in DES. In contrast, the DES rabbit groups with Lys-CNGs-260 topically administered at one dose per 12 h showed abundant goblet cells with purple cytoplasm, similar to those found in the Ctrl groups, indicating that Lys-CNGs-260 promoted ocular surface regeneration. The groups treated with one dose of Lys-CNGs-260 per 48 and 96 h also showed an increase in the number of goblet cells on postoperative day 4 compared to the DES groups (p<0.05) (Fig. 6 B). The DES recovery and ocular regeneration were further evaluated by immunofluorescence staining of MUC5AC, one of the most abundant mucin secreted by conjunctival goblet cells [4,64] (Fig. 6 C). Except for the DES groups, all other groups exhibited green fluorescence, indicating the MUC5AC secretion by goblet cells. The Lys-CNGs-260-treated groups showed much higher MUC5AC expression levels than that of CsA groups (p<0.05) (Fig. 6 D). The results further confirmed that Lys-CNGs-260 enhanced the production of mucin in animal model DES, revealing their high cytoprotective ability. The findings of improvement in tear film stability are highly correlated with goblet cell density, mucin secretion (Fig. 6), rose bengal staining scores (Fig. 4 B), and Schirmer's test (Fig. S10, Supporting Information), suggesting that Lys-CNGs-260 is effective in alleviating DES in rabbit model even with one dose.

Aqueous deficient dry eye (ADDE) is caused mainly due to the decreased secretion of tears from the lacrimal glands, resulting in high osmotic pressure and unstable tear film that dry the ocular surface [65]. Evaporative dry eye (EDE) is due to the deficient tear film lipid layer, which is required to control evaporation and maintain a normal tear film [65]. In the early stages of the disease, EDE can cause excessive tear evaporation, increased tear osmotic pressure, and tear film instability, resulting in symptoms similar to ADDE [66]. Both EDE and ADDE lead to the loss of goblet cells and consequently the reduction of mucin. We believe the high retention of Lys-CNGs on the ocular surface and their superior antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects can alleviate both ADDE and EDE, as the core mechanism of DES is inflammation [67].

We have developed a simple pyrolysis method to prepare bioactive nanogels, Lys-CNGs, from lysine hydrochloride. The obtained Lys-CNGs exhibited good antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities, strong mucoadhesive properties, and high biocompatibility, leading to desirable therapeutic outcome for DES treatment without the need for additional drug loading. The mucoadhesive Lys-CNGs can prolong the residence time on the ocular surface of dry eye animal models, thereby reducing the dosage and frequency of administration. Accordingly, topical Lys-CNGs are more effective than commercial CsA eye drops for DES treatment. We strongly believe that the multifunctional Lys-CNGs can alleviate DES and restore the corresponding ocular surface damage, holding great potential as long-lasting eye drops for dry eye medications in the future. However, a detailed investigation is required on their long-term effect on ocular safety, pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of Lys-CNGs in animal models.

All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this article or supplementary information files. The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.
